title: To further protect their constituents, state lawmakers – Senator Jim Nielsen (R-Tehama) and Assemblyman James Gallagher (R-Yuba City) – who represent the Town of Paradise and Camp

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "PG&E is currently spending roughly $2.4 billion annually to uphold a legislative mandate to buy renewable power.",
    "In 2017, PG&E spent only $1.5 billion to update its century-old infrastructure.",
    "A century-old transmission tower was cited as the ignition source of the Camp Fire.",
    "A utility tower reportedly may also be the source of the Kincade Fire currently burning in Sonoma.",
    "The legislation will temporarily pause the state’s renewable power mandates until infrastructure and vegetation management conditions are improved.",
    "Savings from the temporary relief may only be used by Investor Owned Utilities to harden the grid and reduce forest fuels.",
    "Executive compensation at Investor Owned Utilities would be frozen during the time the renewable power mandates are paused.",
    "Every dollar spent on the additional cost of renewable energy is one dollar that is not available to be spent on vegetation management, line insulation, undergrounding, and other grid-hardening measures.",
    "Dollars spent on forestry management have been found to do more to reduce carbon than other measures.",
    "Redirecting funding to forestry management gets us a better bang for our buck in carbon reduction.",
    "Reports show that some of PG&E’s pre-2012 solar contracts now cost the company an added $2.2 billion per year due to outdated above-market prices.",
    "Savings from renegotiating bloated contracts could be used to invest in projects that reduce fire danger and future blackout events.",
    "Last year’s fires generated 45 million metric tons of carbon, which is more than nine times California’s combined reductions achieved in 2016 and 2017."
  ]
}
```
